verdict: 2026-07-19 09:10:35 PDT — The approved high-level shape is sound: a plan-only skill, referenced recon, canonical JSON with a derived consult view, conventional drift linting/wiring, and a loud engine stop. I would still revise the declarative core before treating v1 as the future engine’s stable contract: rule precedence is internally inconsistent, told overrides are not fully expressible or replayable, and the schema does not enforce several invariants it claims to own.

findings.title: Table P mixes incompatible resolution models
findings.locus: .claude/skills/deep-audit/SKILL.md — Step 3 / Table P and collision rule
findings.claim: P1–P9 emit or upgrade depth candidates under a highest-depth-wins collision rule, while P10 says to downgrade every L1 row. A downgrade cannot win that reducer: the original higher-depth candidate remains, so P10 either has no effect or requires an unstated ordered mutation exception. The central rule table is therefore declarative in appearance but still needs procedural interpretation.
findings.alternative: Use explicit phases: emit baseline candidates, resolve upgrades by maximum depth, then apply named post-resolution transforms such as the mature-repo downgrade in a declared order. Alternatively, remove P10 if maturity should never override risk signals.
findings.win: Eliminates one special-case interpretation from every compiler implementation and gives P10 one mechanically determined result without changing the rest of Table P.

findings.title: The told-override contract cannot express or replay all approved edits
findings.claim: `<lens>:<depth>` only sets existing rows, so it cannot enable a lens omitted by Table P; the smoke artifact nevertheless tells Thomas to add absent security coverage with `security-data-loss:standard`. Step 7 separately permits direct row changes, but those changes are not normalized into the override grammar and the schema records only free-form token/effect prose. An approved plan can therefore cease to be reproducible from repository state plus recorded overrides, weakening the compiled-plan seam the engine will inherit.
findings.alternative: Define one normalized patch model for every consult edit: selector (lens/altitude/scope), operation (add/set/remove), and depth, with explicit expansion over Table L when a selected row is absent. Parse CLI tokens into those structured patches and serialize direct consult edits through the same representation.
findings.win: Creates one replayable told seam, makes the documented security opt-in actually work, removes the parallel direct-edit path, and lets later engine/differential work reconstruct why every approved row exists.

findings.title: The canonical schema does not own the catalog and pricing invariants
findings.claim: The schema independently enumerates lenses and all L0–L3 altitudes but does not encode Table L applicability, so it accepts combinations such as hidden-failure at L3 or architecture-coherence at L1. It also accepts negative counts, duplicate `(lens, altitude, scope)` identities, and totals unrelated to row sums. Consequently, successful JSON-Schema validation does not establish that an artifact is executable under the skill’s declared contract; those invariants remain duplicated in prose and ad hoc smoke checks.
findings.alternative: Encode declarative constraints that JSON Schema can own—lens-specific altitude variants, positive counts, non-empty strings, and date format—and add one small semantic validation step for composite uniqueness and row/total arithmetic that Draft 7 cannot express. Treat that combined validation as the canonical contract check before generating the consult view.
findings.win: Rejects malformed schedules before approval, centralizes lens applicability instead of duplicating it across Table L and consumer code, and removes corresponding defensive branches from the future engine.
